title: End of module: Modern AI: Transformers and Large Language Models

questions.text: In a word embedding, what determines whether two words end up close together in vector space?
choices: They have the same number of letters
They are used in similar contexts / have similar meanings
They appear close together alphabetically
They are both nouns, regardless of meaning
questions.explanation: Embeddings are learned so that words used in similar contexts (and therefore with related meanings) end up with similar vectors, hence close together in the vector space — spelling, alphabetical order, and part of speech alone do not determine closeness.

questions.text: Two toy 2-D word embeddings are king = (4.0, 3.0) and queen = (4.2, 3.5). Using the distance formula $d=\sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2+(y_1-y_2)^2}$, compute the distance between them (2 decimal places).
In the attention mechanism, what does it mean for one word to "attend strongly" to another word in the same sentence?
choices: The model assigns that other word a high relevance weight when building the first word's representation
The two words must be adjacent to each other
The two words must be synonyms
Attention only ever looks at the very first word of the sentence
questions.explanation: Attention computes a relevance score between a word and every other word in the sequence; "attending strongly" means that other word receives a high weight and therefore contributes a lot to the first word's updated, context-aware representation.

questions.text: Which of the following are true about the attention mechanism in transformers? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Each word computes a relevance score against every other word in the sequence
Attention only ever compares exactly two words at a time, never more
Attention weights can help resolve what a pronoun such as "it" or "they" refers to
Attention requires the sequence to be processed strictly left to right, one word at a time
questions.explanation: Attention scores every word against every other word in the whole sequence simultaneously (not just pairs in isolation, and not strictly left-to-right like an RNN); using those scores to blend in the most relevant context is exactly how attention resolves references such as pronouns.

questions.text: Compared to older recurrent neural network (RNN) approaches to language, a core advantage of the transformer architecture is that it…
choices: Processes the words one at a time in strict left-to-right sequence
Processes the whole input sequence in parallel using attention, rather than word-by-word recurrence
Requires no training data at all
Can only ever process a single word as input
questions.explanation: An RNN must finish word $n-1$ before starting word $n$, which is slow and lets long-range information fade. A transformer's attention layers look at the whole sequence at once, enabling parallel computation and direct long-range connections between any two words.

questions.text: What is the general name for the small units — often whole words or word-pieces — into which text is split before being fed into a language model?
questions.explanation: Text is split into **tokens** (e.g. "unhappiness" might become "un" + "happi" + "ness"); the model reads and generates one token at a time.

questions.text: During the "pretraining" phase of building a large language model, what is the model typically trained to do?
choices: Follow human instructions perfectly on the first try
Predict the next word/token given the preceding text, over huge amounts of text
Classify photographs into categories
Memorise a small, hand-picked list of question-answer pairs
questions.explanation: Pretraining repeatedly asks the model to predict the next token given everything before it, across enormous amounts of ordinary text — no human labelling is needed, since the "correct answer" is simply whatever token came next in the real text.

questions.text: Fine-tuning a pretrained language model typically involves…
choices: Starting training over again from randomly initialised weights
Further training on a smaller, curated set of examples (e.g. instructions paired with good responses)
Deleting the attention layers and replacing them with lookup tables
Only ever increasing the size of the vocabulary
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning continues training the already-pretrained model on a much smaller, curated dataset (such as instruction/response pairs), reshaping its behaviour toward being a useful, instruction-following assistant.

questions.text: Which of the following are well-known limitations of large language models? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Hallucination — confidently generating plausible-sounding but false statements
Guaranteed factual accuracy on every answer they give
Sensitivity to exactly how a prompt is phrased
Complete immunity to biases present in their training data
questions.explanation: LLMs are known to hallucinate false but fluent-sounding statements and to be sensitive to prompt phrasing; they are neither guaranteed to be factually accurate nor immune to reproducing biases from their training data — the opposite of options B and D is true.

questions.text: Why do LLMs sometimes "hallucinate" — that is, state false information confidently?
choices: They deliberately try to deceive the user
They generate text by statistical pattern completion rather than by checking claims against real-world facts
They always copy text verbatim from their training data, including its errors
Hallucination only happens if the input prompt itself contains a spelling mistake
questions.explanation: An LLM produces whatever continuation its training makes statistically likely-sounding; it has no built-in mechanism to verify a claim against reality, so a fluent but false statement can be generated with the same confident tone as a true one.

questions.text: A toy bigram model is trained on exactly these three short sentences: "the cat sat", "the dog sat", "the cat ran". Counting consecutive word-pairs, what would the model predict as the most likely word to follow "the"?
choices: cat
dog
sat
ran
questions.explanation: The pair (the, cat) occurs twice (in "the cat sat" and "the cat ran"), while (the, dog) occurs only once — so a frequency-based bigram model predicts "cat" as the most likely word to follow "the".

questions.text: Using the same three sentences — "the cat sat", "the dog sat", "the cat ran" — how many times does the exact word-pair ("the", "cat") occur as a consecutive bigram?
questions.explanation: "the cat" appears as consecutive words in "the cat sat" and again in "the cat ran", so the bigram ("the", "cat") occurs 2 times.

questions.text: According to the conceptual view of LLMs presented in this module, does a large language model "understand" language the way a human does?
choices: Yes — it has genuine comprehension equivalent to a human's
No — it produces its output by statistical pattern completion learned from training data, without genuine comprehension
Only once it has been fine-tuned on enough examples
Only when it is answering questions about mathematics
questions.explanation: An LLM has no beliefs, goals, or world model in the human sense; it generates text by recognising and continuing statistical patterns learned during training, which is why it can read as if it understands while having no way to check its output against reality.
